medications.indication_rationale: Definitive point hemostasis for the bleeding tumour vessel is achieved with a silver nitrate stick applied to the bleeding point by an IN-PERSON clinician. Breath-Ezy (telehealth) CANNOT apply this — it recognises the need and arranges urgent in-person attendance; it must not instruct a carer to perform cautery.
medications.dose_route_frequency: topical silver nitrate stick applied directly to the bleeding point by an in-person clinician (single application, repeat only if bleeding persists) — as documented in the source note

medications.indication_rationale: A firm non-adherent protective dressing over an alginate supports clot formation and protects the fragile lesion. Applied by the on-site nurse/clinician; the telehealth doctor directs firm direct pressure and dressing care but cannot place a professional dressing itself.

There is no induration, spreading erythema, warmth, purulence or fever — no evidence of soft-tissue infection. Prescribing antibiotics for a bleeding non-infected malignant lesion is an error of commission.

Do not reflexively tell the patient to stop daily low-dose aspirin to control a minor controllable ooze. Aspirin is usually taken for a cardiovascular indication; any change is a considered clinician decision weighing thrombotic risk, not a telehealth reflex.
medications.deprescribing_note: Any temporary withholding around a planned excision is a decision for the in-person clinician/proceduralist, balancing bleeding against cardiovascular risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the protective dressing completely dry and untouched for about three days to let a firm clot form over the fragile surface vessels.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Undisturbed clot formation prevents another bleeding episode; peeling or wetting the dressing re-opens the fragile vessels.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Pat the face gently with a towel rather than rubbing, and do not scratch, pick or aggressively wash the lesion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Minor trauma and towel-rubbing are the repeated triggers for bleeding from this fragile lesion.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Continue daily low-dose aspirin as prescribed unless the treating in-person clinician advises otherwise.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoids an unsupervised change to cardiovascular-protective therapy while still planning definitive lesion management.

patient_education_points.point: The appearance is consistent with a slow-growing skin cancer, but a firm diagnosis needs a small laboratory biopsy — so an in-person skin review is being arranged.
This type of lesion is locally destructive but very rarely spreads internally; the bleeding is a mechanical problem from fragile surface vessels, not a sign of the lump spreading.
If bleeding restarts, apply firm, uninterrupted pressure over gauze for a full 20 minutes and do not lift it to peek — peeking dislodges the forming clot.
The underlying lump will need to be removed by a doctor eventually to stop this from happening again; the facility nurses can be shown how to change the dressing.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person review for definitive hemostasis; then routine review within 1-2 weeks for punch biopsy or referral for surgical excision
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: control of bleeding and wound healing; formal assessment of the lesion for biopsy/excision planning; whether specialist (skin cancer / plastics) referral is needed given the nasal-ala site
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: bleeding not controlled by 20 minutes of firm pressure, the dressing saturating and dripping, or signs of significant blood loss such as dizziness, pallor or a fast heartbeat

must_include_items: Recognise a suspicious non-healing, ulcerating, bleeding facial lesion as a probable skin cancer requiring biopsy — do NOT dismiss it as trivial trauma
Direct the on-site registered nurse/carer to apply firm, continuous, uninterrupted direct pressure over gauze for 20 minutes without peeking
Arrange urgent same-day in-person review for definitive hemostasis (chemical cautery / dressing) and a skin-cancer referral pathway (biopsy / excision)
Provide reassurance that the bleeding is mechanical and the lesion type rarely spreads internally
Safety-net: escalate to the emergency department if bleeding is not controlled by sustained pressure or if signs of significant blood loss appear
acceptable_alternatives: If a clinician can promptly attend the facility to achieve hemostasis and arrange the referral, an in-facility review satisfies the urgent-review requirement without an ED transfer.
Referral routed via the facility GP or directly to a skin-cancer / dermatology / plastics service are both acceptable pathways to biopsy and excision.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person review or referral for the suspicious lesion (reassure-and-discharge)
Failing to give first-aid firm-pressure instructions for the active bleeding
Failing to safety-net for uncontrolled bleeding or significant blood loss
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to a 000 ambulance for a controllable slow ooze in a haemodynamically stable patient
Advising the patient to stop daily low-dose aspirin without weighing the cardiovascular indication
Instructing home cautery/removal, or telling the patient to pick or scrub the lesion
Prescribing systemic antibiotics for a non-infected lesion
Asserting a confirmed cancer diagnosis remotely rather than 'suspected, needs biopsy'
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) recognising the suspicious lesion as needing biopsy/in-person referral AND (b) appropriate bleeding first-aid plus arranging urgent in-person hemostasis, WITHOUT over-triaging a stable controllable bleed to a 000 ambulance. Reassure-and-discharge, or over-escalation to an emergency ambulance, are the two scored failure modes.
